glossary.definition: the standard for developing intelligent computer systems; used as a benchmark because humans solve problems using their acquired knowledge stored in long-term memory which has unlimited capacity.
glossary.sentence: Firstly, in one of the first topics of the study course, we have already found that human intelligence is most commonly used as a standard for the development of intelligent computer systems. In this context, it would be logical to provide an intelligent computer system with knowledge, as people use knowledge to solve problems.

glossary.definition: Much less we know about animal intelligence . It is mostly related to learning ability of such animals as monkeys, dolphins, elephant, rat, or crows. However it is even more challenging to measure animal intelligence than human intelligence, because animal diversity is even greater taking into account their cognitive and perceptual capacities, available sensory organs and the ways how they explore the environment in which they live.
glossary.sentence: Much less we know about animal intelligence . It is mostly related to learning ability of such animals as monkeys, dolphins, elephant, rat, or crows. glossary.definition: Knowledge about plant intelligence is even more limited. It is currently known that plant intelligence is not based on a brain and nervous system (Parise, Gagliano and Souza, 2020) . At the same time, plants possess many forms of intellectual performance, such as: they learn based on repeated practice; they have memory; they are able to percept a number of aspects (shape, colour, smell and sound) at the same time; they have a “sense of place” and an awareness of the neighbourhood. “A plant continually gathers and updates diverse information about its environment, integrates this with information on its present internal state, and then makes decisions that reconcile its well-being with its environment” (Trewavas, 2016)

glossary.definition: In this topic, you will find out what we understand by intelligence, how artificial intelligence is defined, and what is a fundamental approach to assessment of intelligence of a computer system.
glossary.sentence: Topic 1.1 - Definition of Artificial Intelligence To figure out what artificial intelligence is, this concept should be considered in a close connection with our understanding of what is intelligence in general. In this topic, you will find out what we understand by intelligence, how artificial intelligence is defined, and what is a fundamental approach to assessment of intelligence of a computer system.

glossary.definition: Thus, in order to cover the diversity of the existent manifestations of intelligent performance, a general definition of intelligence should encompass the essence of the different types of intelligence; not be limited to any particular set of senses, environments, goals, and any specific kind of intelligence implementation structures; be based on principles which are fundamental and thus unlikely to alter over time; ideally be formally expressed, objective, and practically realisable as an effective intelligence test. Unfortunately, the general definition of intelligence does not exist because we, people, still have sufficiently limited knowledge of the world in which we live.

glossary.definition: Systems that think humanly are based on a cognitive modelling approach. It considers human being to be an information processing system. Therefore, research works focus on how information is represented, processed, transformed, and stored in the human mind and in computers.
glossary.sentence: Each category of definitions of artificial intelligence is based on a specific approach used to develop intelligent computer systems (Russell and Norvig, 2009) : A cognitive modelling approach underlies systems that think humanly. It considers human being to be an information processing system. Therefore, research works focus on how information is represented, processed, transformed, and stored in the human mind and in computers.

glossary.definition: The categories use two standards of intelligent performance (Russell and Norvig, 2009) : the categories on the left include definitions that adopt human intelligence as a standard; the categories on the right encompass definitions that adopt an ideal intelligence called rationality as a standard.
glossary.sentence: Meanwhile, Stuart Russell and Peter Norvig (2009) have grouped definitions of artificial intelligence into four categories. Human intelligence is highlighted in two of them. These categories are shown in Figure 2. The previously mentioned categories of definitions focus on different aspects of intelligent performance (Russell and Norvig, 2009): the categories at the top include definitions related to thought processes and reasoning; the categories at the bottom encompass definitions related to behaviour (Fig. Moreover, the categories use two standards of intelligent performance (Russell and Norvig, 2009) : the categories on the left include definitions that adopt human intelligence as a standard; the categories on the right encompass definitions that adopt an ideal intelligence called rationality as a standard (Fig.4).

glossary.definition: The Turing test underlies systems that act humanly and it is also one of the fundamental tests in the field of artificial intelligence intended to assess if a computer system possesses intelligence. It has the following idea. There are 3 rooms. In the first one there is an artificial intelligence, in the second room - a human being or natural intelligence, and in the third room there is a tester (Fig.5). The tester does not see nor artificial intelligence, nor human being and asks questions to both intelligences using a text-based input tool. If the tester cannot differentiate which answers were given by the human being and which ones by the artificial intelligence, than the artificial intelligence is intelligent.
glossary.sentence: The Turing test underlies systems that act humanly and it is also one of the fundamental tests in the field of artificial intelligence intended to assess if a computer system possesses intelligence. It has the following idea. There are 3 rooms. In the first one there is an artificial intelligence, in the second room - a human being or natural intelligence, and in the third room there is a tester (Fig.5). The tester does not see nor artificial intelligence, nor human being and asks questions to both intelligences using a text-based input tool. If the tester cannot differentiate which answers were given by the human being and which ones by the artificial intelligence, than the artificial intelligence is intelligent.

glossary.definition: Learning in artificial neural networks is related to modifying weights in some particular way . It means that after processing the input signals in the whole network, the artificial neural network receives a final output which in the case of supervised machine learning can be compared with the desired (actual label) output. In case if there is a difference between the desired output and the output of the network, a learning rule is applied to modify the weights of the network. Therefore, the artificial neural network starts the next processing step with updated weight values.

glossary.sentence: To pass the Turing test, the computer system must have the following capabilities (Russell and Norvig, 2009) : natural language processing for maintaining successful communication with the tester as the human being does; knowledge representation, in order to store information acquired before and during the test. Humans use for this purpose long-term memory to store all previously acquired knowledge and short-term memory - to store knowledge processed at a specific time; automated reasoning, in order to use the stored information for answering questions and drawing conclusions; machine learning, in order to be able to adapt to a new situation, as well as to acquire and generalize patterns of different situations (for example, to a new topic or intent of the tester, etc.).

glossary.definition: frames allow representing various knowledge about objects including static properties (like inheritance) and executable procedures attached to slots, but they have disadvantages such as being more suitable for static rather than dynamic knowledge, lacking well-defined semantics, and inability to represent certain types of knowledge like negation or disjunction.
glossary.sentence: Frames have the following advantages: frames store knowledge about each object in one place in a structured way; frames provide the hierarchical organization of knowledge; frames allow the representation of various knowledge about objects; frames support inheritance; frames allow adding executable demons to slots to control access to slot values. Frames have disadvantages such as being more suitable for static rather than dynamic knowledge, lacking well-defined semantics, and inability to represent certain types of knowledge.

glossary.sentence: The regular Turing test excludes direct physical interaction between the computer and the tester, because it is not necessary to simulate a human being to make conclusions about intelligence. However, the so called the Total Turing test brings forward two more requirements (Russell and Norvig, 2009) : the computer must have computer vision to perceive objects, and robotics to manipulate objects and move about.

glossary.definition: models intellectual behaviour using explicit symbolic representations of problems and their solutions and rules for transforming these symbols.
glossary.sentence: Symbolic artificial intelligence models intellectual behaviour using explicit symbolic representations of problems and their solutions and rules for transforming these symbols.

glossary.definition: models intellectual behaviour based on distributed representations, learning from data and pattern recognition, without relying on explicit rules and symbolic structures.
glossary.sentence: Connectionist artificial intelligence models intellectual behaviour based on distributed representations, learning from data and pattern recognition, without relying on explicit rules and symbolic structures.

glossary.definition: defined by Newell and Simon in 1976. It defines how the general intelligent performance of both humans and computers is achieved and is formulated as follows: “A physical symbol system has the necessary and sufficient means for general intelligent action” (Newell and Simon, 1976)
glossary.sentence: Symbolic artificial intelligence is based on the physical symbol system hypothesis , defined by Newell and Simon in 1976. It defines how the general intelligent performance of both humans and computers is achieved and is formulated as follows: “A physical symbol system has the necessary and sufficient means for general intelligent action” (Newell and Simon, 1976)

glossary.definition: a system that is capable of carrying out intelligent behaviour only when it is a system of physical symbols. A physical symbol system includes: symbols that correspond to meaningful physical patterns and could represent any form of knowledge, experience, concepts and causality symbolic structures, which consist of symbols that are connected to each other in some way; operations (constructing, copying, modifying, and destroying) which allow the use of existing symbolic structures and the creation of new structures; moreover, the operations themselves can be represented by symbols and symbolic structures in the system.
glossary.sentence: A physical symbol system is capable of carrying out intelligent behaviour only when it is a system of physical symbols (Luger, 2009) . A physical symbol system includes (Bermúdez, 2014; Luger, 2009; Poole and Mackworth, 2010) : symbols that correspond to meaningful physical patterns and could represent any form of knowledge, experience, concepts and causality symbolic structures, which consist of symbols that are connected to each other in some way. Symbolic structures thus depict important aspects of a problem domain; operations (constructing, copying, modifying, and destroying) which allow the use of existing symbolic structures and the creation of new structures; moreover, the operations themselves can be represented by symbols and symbolic structures in the system.

glossary.definition: correspond to meaningful physical patterns and could represent any form of knowledge, experience, concepts and causality. In the context of a computer, symbols are used (e.g., 0 and 1) that when combined into strings represent objects in the world.
glossary.sentence: A physical symbol system includes (Bermúdez, 2014; Luger, 2009; Poole and Mackworth, 2010) : symbols that correspond to meaningful physical patterns and could represent any form of knowledge, experience, concepts and causality ... The computer uses symbols 0 and 1. These symbols are combined into strings of 0 and 1 to represent objects in the world...

glossary.definition: consist of symbols that are connected to each other in some way, depicting important aspects of a problem domain.
glossary.sentence: A physical symbol system includes ... symbolic structures, which consist of symbols that are connected to each other in some way. Symbolic structures thus depict important aspects of a problem domain;

glossary.definition: constructing, copying, modifying, and destroying operations allow the use of existing symbolic structures and the creation of new structures; moreover, these operations themselves can be represented by symbols and symbolic structures in the system.
glossary.sentence: Symbolic structures thus depict important aspects of a problem domain; operations (constructing, copying, modifying, and destroying) which allow the use of existing symbolic structures and the creation of new structures; moreover, the operations themselves can be represented by symbols and symbolic structures in the system.

glossary.definition: symbols that represent meaningful physical patterns and are used to encode knowledge, experience, concepts and causality.
glossary.sentence: A physical symbol system is capable of carrying out intelligent behaviour only when it is a system of physical symbols (Luger, 2009) . A physical symbol system includes ... symbols that correspond to meaningful physical patterns...

glossary.definition: a system comprised of one or more artificial neurons connected in some pattern; processing units or nodes send signals to each other, with input layers receiving environmental inputs and output layers sending final results. The network uses summation and activation functions for computation and can have hidden layers between the input and output layers.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural network An artificial neural network comprises one or more artificial neurons connected in some pattern (Callan, 2003; Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011). Single neurons are called processing units or nodes. They are connected by transmitting signals to each other. You already know that each neuron produces an output. Most neurons in artificial neural networks send this output to other neurons that means that this output becomes an input for other neurons. Every neuron applies the summation and activation functions and receives an output that is sent to other neurons again.

glossary.definition: Although there are many different types of artificial neural networks, in general, they share the following features (Callan, 2003): a set of simple processing units (artificial neurons ).
glossary.sentence: Although there are many different types of artificial neural networks, in general, they share the following features (Callan, 2003): a set of simple processing units (artificial neurons ); a pattern of connectivity of processing units; a function for combining input signals (for example, the summation function ); a function for calculating an output signal (the activation function ); a learning rule to adapt the weights.

glossary.definition: attached to the connections between artificial neurons in an artificial neural network, reflecting the strength of these connections.
glossary.sentence: ... and numerical weights that are attached to the connections between the units, reflecting the strength of these connections.

glossary.term: knowledge is encoded as numerical values
glossary.definition: in connectionist artificial intelligence, knowledge is encoded implicitly in the organisation and interaction of artificial neurons through numerical weights associated with the connections.
glossary.sentence: Knowledge is thus implicitly reflected in the organisation and interaction of artificial neurons, and instead of searching for possible generalisations, connectionist models recognise patterns in the data and reflect them in their structure (Luger, 2009) .

glossary.definition: the process by which a system acquires knowledge by recognizing patterns in data without relying on explicit symbolic rules.
glossary.sentence: Connectionist artificial intelligence models intellectual behaviour based on distributed representations, learning from data and pattern recognition, without relying on explicit rules and symbolic structures.

glossary.definition: the process of identifying regularities or patterns in data that is used by connectionist models to reflect them in their structure.
glossary.sentence: Connectionist artificial intelligence models intellectual behaviour based on distributed representations, learning from data and pattern recognition, without relying on explicit rules and symbolic structures.

glossary.definition: in connectionist artificial intelligence, knowledge is represented as a distributed quantity over a connected group of smaller processing units rather than being encoded in physical symbols.
glossary.sentence: Connectionist artificial intelligence models intellectual behaviour based on distributed representations, learning from data and pattern recognition, without relying on explicit rules and symbolic structures.

glossary.definition: a famous criticism by John Searle describing a scenario in which a person manipulates physical symbols according to an instruction manual without understanding them, thereby demonstrating that correct symbol manipulation does not equate to genuine intelligence.
glossary.sentence: John Searle’s Chinese room argument is a famous example of criticism. Searle describes a situation in which the physical system correctly manipulates physical symbols, but the system has no intelligence.

glossary.definition: the process of transforming physical symbols according to rules as described in the physical symbol system hypothesis; it is also defined as the general intelligent performance achieved by both humans and computers through symbolic manipulation.
glossary.sentence: Symbolic artificial intelligence models intellectual behaviour using explicit symbolic representations ...; Intelligent computer systems based on this paradigm therefore explicitly represent problem domain knowledge (objects and their relations) through a set of symbols. Summarising all of the above, it can be said that 'all intelligent behavior essentially involves transforming physical symbols according to rules' (Bermúdez, 2014).

glossary.definition: the area or subject matter in which the problem is situated, such as medicine, biology and chemistry for a doctor; microeconomics, macroeconomics, financial investments, etc. for a financial specialist; pedagogy, psychology, communication and the specific subject-matter for a teacher.
glossary.sentence: for example, a doctor is able to diagnose diseases because he/she has extensive knowledge in medicine, biology and chemistry; a financial specialist is able to offer investment options because of his/her knowledge of microeconomics, macroeconomics, financial investments, etc.; a faculty’s teacher is able to ensure the study process because he/she has extensive knowledge in pedagogy, psychology, communication and the specific subject-matter,

glossary.definition: knowledge that consists mainly of generalizations available in the relevant problem domain, which are used by symbolic artificial intelligence systems.
glossary.sentence: This knowledge consists mainly of generalisations that are available in the relevant problem domain.

glossary.definition: basic procedures executed by a computer to operate on symbolic structures using symbols such as 0 and 1.
glossary.sentence: The computer creates these structures and operates on them using basic machine language routines.

glossary.definition: programs that use different symbols and symbolic structures to invoke basic procedures in a computer's operation.
glossary.sentence: At the same time, computers also execute programs written in higher-level programming languages. These programmes use different symbols and symbolic structures to invoke basic procedures.

glossary.definition: an example of an artificial intelligence program designed to play the board game Go that combines tree-based search with artificial neural networks, demonstrating the complementary nature of connectionist and symbolic approaches.
glossary.sentence: for example, tree-based search complements artificial neural networks in the AlphaGo program, which is designed to play the board game Go (Goel, 2021).

glossary.definition: a method used in symbolic artificial intelligence that involves exploring possible moves or solutions by traversing a tree of possibilities.
glossary.sentence: for example, tree-based search complements artificial neural networks in the AlphaGo program...

glossary.definition: that is also called immature or limited artificial intelligence, is based on the view that intelligence is a set of abilities and is therefore concerned with developing solutions for some specific tasks such as chess playing, medical diagnosis, etc. This is the only approach in which significant progress has been achieved so far.
glossary.sentence: Narrow (weak) artificial intelligence , that is also called immature or limited artificial intelligence, is based on the view that intelligence is a set of abilities and is therefore concerned with developing solutions for some specific tasks such as chess playing, medical diagnosis, etc. ( Pennachin and Goertzel , 2007) . This is the only approach in which significant progress has been achieved so far.

glossary.definition: or, in another words, strong artificial intelligence, which is also called human-level intelligence (Russell and Norvig, 2020) , is an approach treats intelligence as a general capability that can be copied/replicated (Callan, 2003) . It is related to the development of such computer systems that are capable of solving a wide range of tasks in different problem domains and controlling themselves autonomously with their own thoughts, worries, feelings, strengths, weaknesses and predispositions ( Pennachin and Goertzel , 2007) . Thus, this approach attempts to achieve a level of intelligent performance of an intelligent computer system where the system is able to solve a wide range of tasks, including novel tasks, and perform them as well as a human (Russell and Norvig, 2020) . The following necessary characteristics are defined for artificial general intelligence ( Pennachin and Goertzel , 2007) : the ability to solve general problems in a non-domain-restricted way, in the same sense that a human can; the ability to solve problems in particular domains and particular contexts with particular efficiency; the ability to use its more generalized and more specialized intelligence capabilities together, in a unified way; the ability to learn from its environment, other intelligent systems, and teachers (those, who teach such a kind of systems ); the ability to become better at solving novel types of problems as it gains experience with them.
glossary.sentence: Artificial general intelligence or, in another words, strong artificial intelligence, which is also called human-level intelligence (Russell and Norvig, 2020) , is an approach treats intelligence as a general capability that can be copied/replicated (Callan, 2003) . It is related to the development of such computer systems that are capable of solving a wide range of tasks in different problem domains and controlling themselves autonomously with their own thoughts, worries, feelings, strengths, weaknesses and predispositions ( Pennachin and Goertzel , 2007) . Thus, this approach attempts to achieve a level of intelligent performance of an intelligent computer system where the system is able to solve a wide range of tasks, including novel tasks, and perform them as well as a human (Russell and Norvig, 2020) . The following necessary characteristics are defined for artificial general intelligence ( Pennachin and Goertzel , 2007) : the ability to solve general problems in a non-domain-restricted way, in the same sense that a human can; the ability to solve problems in particular domains and particular contexts with particular efficiency; the ability to use its more generalized and more specialized intelligence capabilities together, in a unified way; the ability to learn from its environment, other intelligent systems, and teachers (those, who teach such a kind of systems ); the ability to become better at solving novel types of problems as it gains experience with them.

glossary.definition: is an intelligence that surpasses human capabilities (Russell and Norvig, 2020) and therefore it is much smarter than the best human minds in practically every field, including scientific creativity, general wisdom and social skills (Bostrom, 2015) .
glossary.sentence: Artificial super intelligence is an intelligence that surpasses human capabilities (Russell and Norvig, 2020) and therefore it is much smarter than the best human minds in practically every field, including scientific creativity, general wisdom and social skills (Bostrom, 2015) .

glossary.definition: focusing on a wide range of organisms, processes, and phenomena that occur at multiple spatial and temporal scales ( Floreano and Mattiussi, 2008). Practitioners of new artificial intelligence assert that true intelligence involves the ability to function in a real-world environment (Copeland, 2018; Pfeifer and Scheier, 2001) . Brooks (1990) , the founder of this approach, has emphasised that the new artificial intelligence is based on the hypothesis that in order to build an intelligent system, it is necessary that their representations are grounded in the physical world, since the world is the best model of itself; in this case, of course, it is necessary to equip the system with sensors in order for the system to perceive the environment and actuators in order for the system to be able to operate in the environment. The main idea is that intelligence is a capability that arises when agents with simpler behaviours interact in the real environment and together achieve more complex behaviours (Copeland, 2018) .
glossary.sentence: "It focuses on a wide range of organisms, processes, and phenomena that occur at multiple spatial and temporal scales" ( Floreano and Mattiussi, 2008). Practitioners of new artificial intelligence assert that true intelligence involves the ability to function in a real-world environment (Copeland, 2018; Pfeifer and Scheier, 2001) . Brooks (1990) , the founder of this approach, has emphasised that the new artificial intelligence is based on the hypothesis that in order to build an intelligent system, it is necessary that their representations are grounded in the physical world, since the world is the best model of itself; in this case, of course, it is necessary to equip the system with sensors in order for the system to perceive the environment and actuators in order for the system to be able to operate in the environment. The main idea is that intelligence is a capability that arises when agents with simpler behaviours interact in the real environment and together achieve more complex behaviours (Copeland, 2018) .

glossary.definition: is a paradigm under new artificial intelligence. It considers collective intelligence as a behaviour that emerges through the interaction and cooperation of large numbers of lesser intelligent agents. It includes ant colony optimization , which studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food, and particle swarm optimization that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish (Brownlee, 2011) . These two sub-directions are used in search and optimisation.
glossary.sentence: Swarm intelligence is a paradigm under new artificial intelligence. It considers collective intelligence as a behaviour that emerges through the interaction and cooperation of large numbers of lesser intelligent agents. It includes ant colony optimization , which studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food, and particle swarm optimization that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish (Brownlee, 2011) . These two sub-directions are used in search and optimisation.

glossary.definition: are general methods (like general purpose search, logics, syntax-based calculations, automatic reasoning, etc.) which could be applied to a broad set of problems. They do not use knowledge on a problem under consideration.
glossary.sentence: Methods of artificial intelligence can be divided into two groups which could not be confused with the approaches of artificial intelligence considered before (Russell and Norvig, 2020; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011; Brownlee, 2011) : weak methods use general methods (like general purpose search, logics, syntax-based calculations, automatic reasoning, etc.) which could be applied to a broad set of problems. They do not use knowledge on a problem under consideration;

glossary.definition: are based on the great amount of knowledge about a problem under consideration and, thus, they take into account problem solving context; strong methods typically use weak methods.
glossary.sentence: strong methods are based on the great amount of knowledge about a problem under consideration and, thus, they take into account problem solving context; strong methods typically use weak methods.

glossary.definition: a complete state space graph; label the levels of the graph as MIN and MAX; assign heuristic measures to all leaf nodes according to an analysis of who wins at a specific node; propagate heuristic measures of leaf nodes up to the root node of the state space graph according to the following rules: a) if a node is located at MAX level, it acquires the maximum heuristic measure from among its direct descendants, and b) if a node is located at MIN level, it acquires the minimum heuristic measure from among its direct descendants; determine the result of the game and the winning paths.
glossary.sentence: It involves the following steps (Luger, 2009): create a complete state space graph; label the levels of the graph as MIN and MAX; assign heuristic measures to all leaf nodes according to an analysis of who wins at a specific node; propagate heuristic measures of leaf nodes up to the root node of the state space graph according to the following rules: ... determine the result of the game and the winning paths.

glossary.definition: a mathematical or formal specification that uses mathematical concepts to represent objects of the real world and their behavior in a way that they can be easily transformed into computational objects. It is used as an intermediate between the real world and intelligent computer systems.

glossary.term: deterministic two-player game of perfect information
glossary.definition: A deterministic two-player game of perfect information is one where moves are made alternately by players, and every move is fully known to both players. The state space graph for such games has clearly distinguishable node levels because only one player can make a move from states at any given level.

glossary.definition: Parameters characterize the states of a game. They are related to a computer implementation of a game; they correspond to the properties of the game and its course (values change after moves), but not to characteristics of the state space graph like number of nodes, levels, or arcs. In the oversimplified game, parameters include the numerical string, score of first player, and score of second player.
glossary.sentence: As a result, before creating a state space graph, the developer of an intelligent computer system should determine the parameters that characterise the states in the game . These parameters can be described as follows: they are related to a computer implementation of a game; they correspond to the properties of the game and its course, in which the values change after making a move during the course of the game; they are not related to the characteristics of the state space graph, such as the number of nodes, the number of levels, the number of arcs, etc.; the values of the parameters allow the players to track the course of the game, since they determine a specific state in the game at a specific moment in time; the values of the parameters should be represented in each node of the state space graph; the parameters depend on the game, and thus may be very different; for example, they may include the score of each player, the current game board, or both the scores of the players and the current game board, etc.

glossary.definition: a type of search in rule-based expert systems that begins with a problem description or given data about the problem (a pattern) that are added to the working memory; it matches the pattern against the IF part of production rules, fires rules by adding their THEN parts to the working memory, and continues until the search goal is found
glossary.sentence: In both cases, the search starts from the first rule in the knowledge base, and only a way how the rules are explored differs. In data-driven search (Luger, 2005; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): the search begins with a problem description or given data about the problem (a pattern) that are added to the working memory; the control cycle 'Recognize-act' matches the pattern against the IF part of production rules in the knowledge base; firing a rule, its THEN part is added to the working memory, and the process continues; searching stops when the search goal is found.

glossary.definition: a technique for gathering information and then inferring from it whatever can be inferred ( Negnevitsky , 2011), used in data-driven search where most of the data are initially given, and the system searches for their interpretation; however, many rules may fire that have nothing to do with the established goal
glossary.sentence: Figure 3 represents the idea of forward chaining. Forward chaining is a technique for gathering information and then inferring from it whatever can be inferred ( Negnevitsky , 2011). It is typically used for problems of interpretation because, in this type of problems, most of the data are initially given, and the system searches for their interpretation (Luger, 2009; Hopgood, 2001). However, the intelligent computer system may execute many rules in forward chaining that have nothing to do with the established goal ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: a type of search in rule-based expert systems where an expert system has a goal (a hypothetical solution), and the inference engine attempts to find evidence to prove it; this involves adding a goal (pattern) to working memory, matching against THEN parts of production rules, firing rules that add IF parts to working memory, continuing until facts about the problem are found
glossary.sentence: In backward chaining , an expert system has the goal (a hypothetical solution), and the inference engine attempts to find the evidence to prove it ( Negnevitsky , 2011). In goal-driven search (Luger, 2005; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): a goal (a pattern) is added to the working memory; the control cycle 'Recognize-act' matches the pattern against the THEN part of production rules in the knowledge base; firing a rule, its IF part is added to the working memory, and the process continues; searching stops when facts about the problem are found.

glossary.sentence: Nodes should be added to both lists together with their depth labels. Depth labels allow the algorithm 'to understand' if it is possible to generate descendants/ancestors of the current node or if the current node should be considered a leaf/root node.

glossary.definition: The depth limit is a predefined boundary that defines how deeply the state space graph can be explored by Depth-Limited Search. Nodes located at the depth limit are considered to be leaf/root nodes (depending on the search direction), and no nodes below this level are expanded.
glossary.sentence: Depth-Limited Search includes an additional operational step... The algorithm checks if its depth is lower than the specified depth limit.

glossary.definition: Leaf/root nodes are those at the depth limit. They are considered to be terminal nodes in the search process because no further expansion occurs below this level.
glossary.sentence: Nodes located at the depth limit are considered to be leaf/root nodes (depending on the search direction).

glossary.definition: a search algorithm that explores the state space graph level-by-level by adding new nodes to the right side of the OPEN list, which is a queue following the 'First in, First Out' principle; it finds the shortest path because it searches level by level.
glossary.sentence: In this way, it explores the state space graph level-by-level (Figure 16)... The search start node is A. ... Breadth-First Search is also guaranteed to find the search goal node if this node exists in the state space graph, as this algorithm performs an exhaustive search of the space state graph. This algorithm is also guaranteed to find the shortest path to the search goal node if such a path exists in the state space graph. The reason is that when searching in a level-by-level fashion, the shortest path will be found sooner because it consists of a smaller number of arcs, which means that the search goal node is located at higher levels.

glossary.definition: a layer or depth in the state space graph; nodes at higher levels have been reached by more arcs, meaning that the path from the start node to these nodes is longer. Breadth-First Search finds the shortest path because it searches level-by-level.
glossary.sentence: Breadth-First Search is also guaranteed to find the search goal node if this node exists in the state space graph... The reason is that when searching in a level-by-level fashion, the shortest path will be found sooner because it consists of a smaller number of arcs, which means that the search goal node is located at higher levels.

glossary.definition: a type of data structure that works according to the 'First in, First Out' principle; in Breadth-First Search, new nodes are added to the right side and processed from the left side.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, this list is a type of data structure called a queue. A queue works according to the “First in, First Out” principle: the first data element entered is the first to be processed (Figure 18).

glossary.definition: a node generated from an expanded node; in the context of Breadth-First Search, when a current node is expanded it generates its descendant nodes which are then added to the OPEN list if they have not been processed before.
glossary.sentence: Iteration 1: The algorithm expands the current node A by generating its three descendants - nodes D, H, and E. ... Iteration 2: The algorithm expands the current node D by generating its descendant - the node J. ... Iteration 3: The algorithm expands the current node H by generating its three descendants - nodes J, I, and E.

glossary.definition: a modification of Breadth-First Search that reduces computational resource consumption by performing searches simultaneously from both the search start node and the search goal node until a common node is found; it uses two OPEN lists and two CLOSED lists, one for each direction.
glossary.sentence: To overcome this shortcoming, a modification of Breadth-First Search is offered. Bidirectional Search is a modification of Breadth-First Search. The algorithm reduces the consumption of computational resources (search time and computer memory), which is the main problem with Breadth-First Search. The algorithm performs a search simultaneously in both directions - data-driven search and goal-driven search - until it finds a point of contact in both directions.

glossary.definition: a connection or edge between nodes in the state space graph; the number of arcs in a path relates to its length, with fewer arcs indicating a shorter path.
glossary.sentence: The reason is that when searching in a level-by-level fashion, the shortest path will be found sooner because it consists of a smaller number of arcs, which means that the search goal node is located at higher levels.

glossary.definition: resources such as time and memory used by an algorithm; Breadth-First Search has a serious shortcoming related to the consumption of computing resources as estimated by Russell and Norvig (2010).
glossary.sentence: Unfortunately, Breadth-First Search has a serious shortcoming related to the consumption of computing resources. Russell and Norvig (2010) have provided time and memory estimates for Breadth-First Search processing a state space graph with an average of 10 descendants per node.

glossary.definition: the set or collection of all possible states represented in the state space graph; it is explored by search algorithms like Breadth-First Search and Bidirectional Search.
glossary.sentence: In this way, it explores the state space graph level-by-level (Figure 16). ... Breadth-First Search is also guaranteed to find the search goal node if this node exists in the state space graph, as this algorithm performs an exhaustive search of the space state graph.

glossary.definition: the algorithm goes up one level, and finds that the current node has another unconsidered descendant. It is not possible to assign a precise heuristic measure in this case, and the algorithm is able to assign only an intermediate heuristic measure of >=−1, since the node is located at the MAX level and the only known heuristic measure is a measure of −1.
glossary.sentence: As a result, the algorithm goes up one level, and finds that the current node has another unconsidered descendant. It is not possible to assign a precise heuristic measure in this case, and the algorithm is able to assign only an intermediate heuristic measure of >=−1, since the node is located at the MAX level and the only known heuristic measure is a measure of −1.

glossary.definition: knowledge called heuristic knowledge that very often is based on our experience.
glossary.sentence: To solve problems, we use problem-specific knowledge called heuristic knowledge that very often is based on our experience.

glossary.definition: knowledge that very often is based on our experience. In the case of playing games, we have a specific game strategy that allows us to choose the most promising moves to make. In financial management, heuristic knowledge includes knowing favourable conditions for investments or buying currency. Doctors have heuristic knowledge about relationships between different symptoms and diagnoses.
glossary.sentence: To solve problems, we use problem-specific knowledge called heuristic knowledge that very often is based on our experience...Doctors have heuristic knowledge about relationships between different symptoms and diagnoses.

glossary.definition: a function used in both N-ply look-ahead and iterative deepening to evaluate nodes when the entire state space cannot be fully analyzed. It is applied with limited look-ahead, and its results are propagated up using the principles of the minimax algorithm or alpha-beta pruning.
glossary.sentence: One of the most significant problems with this approach is called the horizon effect (Jones, 2009; Luger, 2009). When a heuristic evaluation function is applied with limited look-ahead, it is possible that the depth of the look-ahead may not detect a heuristically promising path... Both strategies described above use a heuristic evaluation function.

glossary.definition: Values assigned to nodes, either directly using simple values (+1, 0, -1) or via a heuristic evaluation function, which indicate the desirability or outcome of game states.
glossary.sentence: Assigning heuristic measures In both cases, the intelligent computer system assigns heuristic measures to the nodes of the available graph, and these allow the intelligent computer system to select promising moves to make during game play.

glossary.definition: the algorithmic procedure where descendants of a current node in the state space graph are generated and evaluated to determine promising moves.
glossary.sentence: Imagine that in the search process, the algorithm has generated three descendants of the current node C (Figure 2).

glossary.definition: the distance between the search start node and node n; it allows the algorithm to choose shorter paths available.
glossary.sentence: f(n) = g(n) + h(n), where n – a node under consideration; g(n) – the distance between the search start node and node n;

glossary.definition: a heuristic measure that characterizes the quality of node n or, in other words, the estimation of the distance between the node n and the search goal node; it allows the algorithm to choose promising paths in a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: f(n) = g(n) + h(n), where ... h(n) – a heuristic measure that characterizes the quality of node n or, in other words, the estimation of the distance between the node n and the search goal node;

glossary.definition: a game used to demonstrate the idea of problem-specific factors and their numerical representation. The system has a state space graph of the game, and the intelligent computer system selects moves based on heuristic evaluation functions such as the number of potentially winning positions.
glossary.sentence: Let’s consider examples of two games demonstrating the idea of problem-specific factors and their numerical representation. Imagine we are building an intelligent computer system that will play a game of Tic-tac-toe.

glossary.definition: a factor used in heuristic evaluation function for Tic-tac-toe, where states where the computer can place its sign in a corner receive a value of 3 and centre receives a value of 4, indicating more promising moves.
glossary.sentence: To evaluate nodes in the graph, the system can apply a heuristic evaluation function based on such a factor as the number of potentially winning positions for the computer. ... Any state where the computer can place its sign in a corner receives a value of 3 as there are 3 potentially winning positions. The state where the computer can place its sign in the centre receives a value of 4 as there are 4 potentially winning positions.

glossary.definition: a game involving 8 numbered tiles and 9 squares, where players move a tile onto an empty square with the aim of achieving the goal state. The heuristic evaluation for this game considers factors like the number of tiles out of place.
glossary.sentence: Another example is the game of 8-puzzle. There are 8 numbered tiles and 9 squares. Players are allowed to move a tile onto an empty square with the aim of achieving the goal state that you can see in Figure 5.

glossary.definition: a factor used to evaluate nodes in the 8-puzzle game, where the number of tiles not in their correct positions compared to the goal state is counted. The state with fewer misplaced tiles is considered closer to the goal state.
glossary.sentence: We will consider only three states generated from the initial state of the game. The simplest factor to consider is the number of the tiles out of place in each state when it is compared with the goal state (Figure 6). The logic behind such a heuristic evaluation function is quite simple: the state that has the fewest tiles out of place is probably closer to the goal state.

glossary.definition: another factor used in evaluating nodes for the 8-puzzle game, which sums up the distances of tiles from their intended places. A smaller sum indicates a state that is closer to achieving the goal state.
glossary.sentence: Another factor that can be used to evaluate nodes is to sum up distances of tiles from their intended places (Figure 7). The logic is also very similar here: the smaller the sum of distances, the closer a state is to the search goal node.

glossary.definition: a factor considered in evaluating nodes for the 8-puzzle game that counts the number of pairs of tiles that are reversed relative to their intended order. Changing places of two adjacent tiles usually takes more than two moves, so fewer reversals indicate a state closer to the goal.
glossary.sentence: We can define one more factor for this game: the number of pairs of tile reversals (Figure 8). This means that we need to change the places of two tiles located close one to another, and usually, it takes more than two moves to put them back. In our case, all three nodes will receive a value of 0, because there are no such situations on the board.

glossary.definition: an element or component used in the heuristic evaluation function that characterizes states in the problem-solving process. Factors can be combined with weights to form complex functions for evaluating nodes.
glossary.sentence: We have found three different factors for the same game (Figure 9). We can use each of them alone as a heuristic evaluation function, ... and we can add weights if we know that some factors are more important than others: f(n) = weight_1*the number of tiles out of place + weight_2*the sum of distances + weight_3*the number of tile reversals.

glossary.definition: a numerical coefficient assigned to a factor in the heuristic evaluation function, indicating its relative importance. For example, weight_1 for the number of tiles out of place, weight_2 for the sum of distances, and weight_3 for the number of tile reversals.
glossary.sentence: We can add weights if we know that some factors are more important than others: f(n) = weight_1*the number of tiles out of place + ... etc.

glossary.definition: the elements included in the heuristic evaluation function whose number and computational cost must be balanced; factors that characterize problem-solving states, indicate search progress towards goal node, or judge if a node is on the shortest path to the search goal node.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, when developing the heuristic evaluation function, the following aspects should be taken into account : Which factors characterize problem-solving states in a state space graph? Which of the identified factors could indicate that the search is moving towards the search goal node? Which of the identified factors could be used to judge if a node under consideration is located on the shortest path to the search goal node?

glossary.definition: a problem where when a heuristic evaluation function is applied with limited look-ahead, it is possible that the depth of the look-ahead may not detect a heuristically promising path, and this may lead to a bad situation later in the game.
glossary.sentence: One of the most significant problems with this approach is called the horizon effect (Jones, 2009; Luger, 2009). When a heuristic evaluation function is applied with limited look-ahead, it is possible that the depth of the look-ahead may not detect a heuristically promising path, and this may lead to a bad situation later in the game.

glossary.definition: the average number of arcs per node in a state space graph; usually acquired by dividing the sum of direct descendants of all nodes with the total number of nodes.
glossary.sentence: In general, the complexity of a state space graph depends on two factors (Luger, 2009): B – the branching factor of the state space graph . It is the average number of arcs per node. Usually, we acquire this factor by dividing the sum of direct descendants of all nodes with the total number of nodes in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: the length of the longest path in a state space graph; sometimes estimated approximately if exact numbers are unknown.
glossary.sentence: L – depth of the state space graph (usually the length of the longest path).

glossary.definition: a method to reduce the complexity of a state space graph by transforming it before its use in an intelligent computer system; for example, using symmetry features such as in Tic-tac-toe.
glossary.sentence: What can we do if we know that the state space graph is large? One of the methods is to transform a state space graph before its use by the intelligent computer system. Unfortunately, there are no clear guidelines on how to make such a transformation.

glossary.definition: a characteristic used in transforming state space graphs to reduce complexity; for instance, considering only three states (corner, centre, and side) at the first level of the Tic-tac-toe game tree due to symmetry.
glossary.sentence: One of the suggestions is to use a symmetry feature of states, which can be done for the game of Tic-tac-toe.

glossary.definition: the overall count of nodes in a state space graph; it depends on the interdependence of branching factor and depth as presented by Figure 11.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, the total number of nodes (T) is The interdependence of the branching factor (B), the depth (L), and the total number of nodes (T) is presented by the plot that you can see in Figure 11.

glossary.definition: the rapid increase in the number of nodes when exploring all paths to a given depth; for example, with branching factor equal to 2 and increased depth, node count grows exponentially.
glossary.sentence: If the branching factor of the state space graph is equal to 2 (nodes have on average 2 descendants), then exploring all paths to a depth of 6 demands visiting more than 100 nodes. However, by doubling the depth (12), the number of nodes to visit is 10 times larger (8190 nodes). So, there is exponential growth.

glossary.definition: The subject matter discussed in the text.
glossary.sentence: Topic 3.2 - Heuristic Search Algorithms

glossary.definition: The module in which uninformed search algorithms were considered previously.
glossary.sentence: In Module 2 of our course, we considered the operational steps of uninformed search algorithms.

glossary.definition: The main actions performed by an algorithm during its execution. For uninformed search algorithms, these include selecting the current node, checking if the current node is the goal, and expanding the current node if it's not.
glossary.sentence: They have three main operational steps (Figure 1): selecting the current node; checking if the current node is the search goal node; expanding the current node if it is not the search goal node.

glossary.definition: A heuristic search algorithm that always moves to the best node (highest or lowest depending on definition) in the state space graph. It does not implement backtracking and stops when a goal is reached or no better node is found.
glossary.sentence: Let’s consider specific heuristic search algorithms. The idea of the Hill Climbing algorithm is to always move to the best node in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: Used as an analogy for a state space graph where nodes have heuristic measures; the goal node is at the top and the start node at the bottom.
glossary.sentence: If we imagine that the state space graph with heuristic measures of nodes is similar to a mountain, then the search goal node can be considered to be the top of the mountain, while the search start node is the foot of the mountain (Figure 4).

glossary.definition: A shortcoming of the Hill Climbing algorithm where it fails to reach the goal because it finds a node with better heuristic measure than its descendants, leading to a dead end.
glossary.sentence: the local maximum problem : the algorithm does not reach the search goal node because it finds a node that has a better heuristic measure than the heuristic measures of its descendants/ancestors (depending on the search direction );

glossary.definition: A shortcoming of Hill Climbing where the algorithm cannot progress if no descendant or ancestor has a strictly better heuristic measure than the current node.
glossary.sentence: the plateau problem : the algorithm cannot continue the search if the heuristic measure of its best descendant/ancestor (depending on the search direction) is equal to the heuristic measure of the current node.

glossary.definition: A heuristic search algorithm that selects the best node found so far but retains all nodes in the OPEN list. It expands only one node at a time from the OPEN list and reorders them based on their heuristic measures, allowing it to switch between different paths.
glossary.sentence: The Best-First Search algorithm does not have these shortcomings. Let’s consider it. In each iteration, Best-First Search selects the best node found so far, but it also keeps all of the nodes that were found before.

glossary.definition: A data structure used to implement the OPEN list in Best-First Search; it orders nodes based on their heuristic measures so that the best node is processed first.
glossary.sentence: The OPEN list is implemented as a data structure called a priority queue (Figure 7). The priority queue works according to the principle that the node with the best heuristic measure is processed first.

glossary.definition: An iteration refers to a single cycle or step in the algorithm's process, where a node is selected from the OPEN list and expanded to generate descendants.
glossary.sentence: Iteration 0: The search start node is added ...; Iteration 1: The algorithm expands the current node A ...

glossary.definition: Best-First Search is an algorithm that guarantees to find the search goal node if a path exists between the search start node and the search goal node. It uses heuristic measures to determine the order of node expansion.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, Best-First Search is guaranteed to find the search goal node if a path between the search start node and the search goal node exists.

glossary.definition: Hill Climbing is mentioned as an algorithm that expands only one node per iteration, in contrast to Best-First Search which stores and incrementally expands all nodes found before.
glossary.sentence: Let’s consider an algorithm providing a kind of compromise between Hill Climbing and Best-First Search.

glossary.definition: Beam Search is described as a compromise between Hill Climbing, which expands only one node per iteration, and Best-First Search, which stores and incrementally expands all nodes found before. It selects a limited number of best nodes for further expansion in each iteration.
glossary.sentence: Let’s consider an algorithm providing a kind of compromise between Hill Climbing and Best-First Search. Beam Search is a compromise between Hill Climbing, ...

glossary.definition: A parameter defined by the algorithm that determines how many of the best nodes (based on heuristic measures) are selected from the OPEN list.
glossary.sentence: According to the defined beam width, the algorithm selects the two best nodes based on heuristic measures.

glossary.definition: Direct descendants or ancestors refer to nodes generated from a current node during expansion. In Beam Search, the algorithm always simultaneously expands all nodes located in the OPEN list at each iteration and evaluates their descendants/ancestors (depending on search direction).
glossary.sentence: The algorithm always simultaneously expands all nodes located in the OPEN list at each iteration, evaluates their descendants/ancestors (depending on a search direction)...

glossary.definition: Figure 9 is referenced as containing the state space graph used for the Beam Search example.
glossary.sentence: Let us consider an example of Beam Search by implementing data-driven search on the state space graph that you can see in Figure 9.

glossary.definition: Search direction refers to whether the search evaluates descendants (forward search) or ancestors (backward search). In Beam Search, the algorithm expands nodes and evaluates their descendants/ancestors depending on the search direction.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm always simultaneously expands all nodes located in the OPEN list at each iteration, evaluates their descendants/ancestors (depending on a search direction)...

glossary.definition: A state space graph that includes all states from the initial state (root node) to all goal states (leaf nodes). It is typically used in simple games or when sufficient computational resources are available.
glossary.sentence: Definition A complete state space graph includes all states, from the initial state of the game (represented by the root node of the graph), to all goal states of the game (represented by leaf nodes).

glossary.definition: A sub-graph of the complete state space graph that usually includes states between a specific node and a predefined depth. It is normally used for complex games or when computational resources are insufficient, or when it is known that the solution lies at a specific depth.
glossary.sentence: Definition A partial state space graph is a sub-graph of the complete state space graph, and usually includes states between a specific node and a predefined depth.

glossary.definition: the task of the maximiser is to win the game. The algorithm assigns a heuristic measure indicating that in the worst case, the maximiser will have a measure of −1, but in the best case, the measure may be higher.
glossary.sentence: This heuristic measure indicates that in the worst case, the maximiser will have a measure of −1, but in the best case, the measure may be higher, since the task of the maximiser is to win the game.

glossary.definition: the minimiser has the highest score and therefore wins the game; also, it indicates that in the worst case, the minimiser will take the measure of −1, but in the best case, the measure will be lower.
glossary.sentence: It reaches a leaf node in which the minimiser has the highest score and therefore wins the game. ... The assigned heuristic measure indicates that in the worst case the minimiser will take the measure of −1, but in the best case, the measure will be lower, since the task of the minimiser is to minimise the game result for the maximiser.

glossary.definition: The division of the state space graph into levels representing alternating moves by the maximiser (labelled as MAX) and the minimiser (labelled as MIN).
glossary.sentence: Dividing the state space graph into levels In both cases, the available graph is alternately divided into levels representing the maximiser (labelled as MAX) and minimiser (labelled as MIN), remembering that moves in two-player games of perfect information are made alternately.

glossary.definition: In a complete state space graph, the heuristic measure assigned to the root node that clearly shows the outcome if both players play rationally; in a partial state space graph, it indicates only the best state reachable after n moves and is not an actual game result.
glossary.sentence: The result of assigning heuristic measures to nodes In the case of a complete state space graph, a heuristic measure assigned to the root node is called a game result. It clearly shows the result of the game if both players play rationally... In the case of a partial state space graph, a heuristic measure assigned to the node representing the current state of the game indicates only the best state that can be reached after making n moves from the current state. It is not a game result!

glossary.definition: In a complete state space graph, a winning path is one that starts at the root node and reaches a leaf node with all nodes having the same heuristic measure equal to the game result; these paths show the most likely course of the game if played rationally.
glossary.sentence: Identifying paths In the case of the complete state space graph, winning paths are identified based on the game result. These are not paths that definitely lead to a win by a particular player; they are paths showing the course of the game that is most likely to be undertaken.

glossary.definition: The minimax algorithm pursues all of the branches and evaluates all of the nodes in the state space graph, and may therefore require significant computational resources. According to Jones (2009), it is a good algorithm for a state space graph with a low depth and branching factor, but can consume large amounts of storage for more complex problems.
glossary.sentence: The minimax algorithm pursues all of the branches and evaluates all of the nodes in the state space graph, and may therefore require significant computational resources. According to Jones (2009), it is a good algorithm for a state space graph with a low depth and branching factor, but can consume large amounts of storage for more complex problems.

glossary.definition: Alpha-beta pruning improves the search efficiency by reducing the number of nodes evaluated and consequently the time spent searching for a solution. It is based on the idea that moves that are not beneficial (or in other words, moves that make no difference to the final decision) can be ignored ... returns the same move as minimax, but cuts off unpromising paths from the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: Alpha-beta pruning improves the search efficiency by reducing the number of nodes evaluated and consequently the time spent searching for a solution. It is based on the idea that moves that are not beneficial (or in other words, moves that make no difference to the final decision) can be ignored ... returns the same move as minimax, but cuts off unpromising paths from the state space graph.

glossary.definition: a strategy for processing a partial state space graph where the algorithm goes down from the current state of the game to the terminal nodes located at the predefined depth, applies a heuristic evaluation function and acquires heuristic measures for the terminal nodes, then propagates these measures back up using the principles of the minimax algorithm.
glossary.sentence: A partial state space graph is a more complex case... N-ply look-ahead explores a limited number of levels denoted by n. Below, we give an abstract example of its operation (Figure 12). The algorithm goes down from the current state of the game to the terminal nodes located at the predefined depth.

glossary.definition: a strategy used when there is a time limit on making the next move in the game. It explores the partial state space graph in an iterative manner – at each iteration, it goes one level deeper and evaluates nodes using a heuristic evaluation function, with the possibility to abort the search at any time based on available time.
glossary.sentence: Iterative deepening can be used when there is a time limit on making the next move... It starts with depth limit of 1, and evaluates nodes at this depth using a heuristic evaluation function. Acquired heuristic measures are propagated up to the current node using the rules of the minimax algorithm or alpha-beta pruning.

glossary.definition: levels of the graph as MIN and MAX; if a node is located at MIN level, it acquires the minimum heuristic measure from among its direct descendants.
glossary.sentence: label the levels of the graph as MIN and MAX; assign heuristic measures to all leaf nodes according to an analysis of who wins at a specific node; propagate heuristic measures of leaf nodes up to the root node ... if a node is located at MIN level, it acquires the minimum heuristic measure from among its direct descendants.

glossary.definition: levels of the graph as MIN and MAX; if a node is located at MAX level, it acquires the maximum heuristic measure from among its direct descendants.
glossary.sentence: label the levels of the graph as MIN and MAX; assign heuristic measures to all leaf nodes according to an analysis of who wins at a specific node; propagate heuristic measures of leaf nodes up to the root node ... if a node is located at MAX level, it acquires the maximum heuristic measure from among its direct descendants.

glossary.definition: a measure that indicates in the worst case, the maximiser will have a measure of −1, but in the best case, the measure may be higher, since the task of the maximiser is to win the game. Also, an intermediate heuristic measure is assigned when it's not possible to assign a precise one; and a precise heuristic measure is acquired based on known measures of direct descendants or terminal nodes.
glossary.sentence: It finds that this node does not have other unconsidered descendants, and therefore acquires a precise heuristic measure of −1. ... The current node is located at the MAX level, but there is no difference between the heuristic measures of the descendants, and the current node therefore acquires a heuristic measure of −1.

glossary.definition: determine the result of the game and winning paths
glossary.sentence: determine the result of the game and the winning paths.

glossary.definition: An alpha number (α) is assigned to nodes at MAX levels. This value should not decrease, since this would represent a bad move. Hence, this number defines the best move that can be made to maximise the result of the game.
glossary.sentence: Alpha-beta pruning uses two numbers: An alpha number (α) is assigned to nodes at MAX levels ...

glossary.definition: A beta number (β) is assigned to nodes at MIN levels. It should not increase, since this would represent a bad move. This number therefore defines the best move that can be done to minimise the result of the game.
glossary.sentence: Alpha-beta pruning uses two numbers: ... A beta number (β) is assigned to nodes at MIN levels. It should not increase, since this would represent a bad move. This number therefore defines the best move that can be done to minimise the result of the game.

glossary.definition: an α-cut-off should be performed by cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node. The algorithm cuts off one unconsidered path.
glossary.sentence: By comparing the measures, the algorithm finds that the value of β is smaller than α, meaning that an α-cut-off should be performed by cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node.

glossary.definition: A procedure performed when the intermediate heuristic measure at a node (α value) equals the β value of its ancestor, resulting in cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm finds that the α value of the current node is equal to the β value of the ancestor, meaning that a β-cut-off should be performed by cutting off the unconsidered paths below the current node. The algorithm cuts off one unconsidered path.

glossary.definition: assigns a precise heuristic measure of “1” to the leaf node, since this is a state where the maximiser wins the game ... assigns a precise heuristic measure that is equal to the heuristic measure of the leaf node; ... assigns a precise heuristic measure in this case (leaf nodes); when a precise heuristic measure has been assigned, the algorithm goes up one level
glossary.sentence: Step 1: The algorithm goes down using the first unconsidered path on the left until it reaches a leaf node. It assigns a precise heuristic measure of “1” to the leaf node ... Step 2: The algorithm assigns a precise heuristic measure that is equal to the heuristic measure of the leaf node ...

glossary.definition: when it is not possible to assign a precise heuristic measure, the algorithm is able to assign only an intermediate heuristic measure (e.g., >=1 for MAX level or <=-1 for MIN level), indicating that in the worst case the maximiser will have a measure of 1, but in the best case the measure will be higher; similarly, for MIN nodes, an intermediate measure indicates that in the worst case the minimiser will have a measure of -1, but in the best case it might be lower.

glossary.definition: the process involved in machine learning where models are built to analyze data.
glossary.sentence: Machine learning is a method of data analysis that automates analytical model building. It is a branch of artificial intelligence based on the idea that systems can learn from data, identify patterns and make decisions with minimal human intervention.”

glossary.definition: the study of computers and computing, including their theoretical and algorithmic foundations, hardware and software, and their uses for processing information.
glossary.sentence: Computer science is the broadest concept in this context. It is “the study of computers and computing, including their theoretical and algorithmic foundations, hardware and software, and their uses for processing information” (Tucker & Belford, 2019).

glossary.definition: one type of machine learning algorithm where the model is trained on labeled data.
glossary.sentence: Machine learning includes three types of algorithms – supervised, unsupervised and reinforcement learning algorithms.

glossary.definition: algorithms that are explicitly programmed by developers, providing exact and complete instructions for each step along with known inputs and outputs, which may lead to errors if unknown input is encountered.
glossary.sentence: The conventional algorithms of computer science are explicitly programmed by their developers. It means that the developers code each step of an algorithm in a precise way together with possible inputs and outputs. Therefore, a conventional algorithm is a set of exact and complete instructions to fulfil a task. Such an algorithm is able to provide an output only to the inputs that are known. As a result, if the algorithm receives an unknown input, it gets into error.

glossary.definition: algorithms in machine learning that do not follow a precise set of instructions; instead, they use general guidelines about a mathematical model and initial configuration variables (hyperparameters) to perform operations on data for tasks like prediction, classification or clustering.
glossary.sentence: Machine learning algorithms are not based on a precise set of instructions. On the contrary, the developers provide only general guidelines about a mathematical model that should be used and set initial configuration variables (hyperparameters) of the model. However, after that, a machine learning algorithm performs mathematical operations to the given data according to the model specified with the aim of finding the best values of parameters to learn a prediction, solve a classification task or find clusters of data.

glossary.definition: the fundamental element in machine learning; they can come from various sources and be represented in many forms (images, text, numbers) and are converted into digital form for analysis.
glossary.sentence: Data is at the heart of machine learning. They can come from different places (like governmental institutions, research institutions, industry, etc.) and be represented in many forms (images, text, numbers, etc.). In machine learning, data are converted into a digital form for further analysis and use in the task.

glossary.definition: The classification task can be divided into two types: Binary classification is when there are only two possible classes/categories which are represented by labels 0 (negative) and 1 (positive ); Multiclass classification is when there are more than two classes to which data objects can belong.
glossary.sentence: The classification task can be divided into two types: Binary classification is when there are only two possible classes/categories which are represented by labels 0 (negative) and 1 (positive ); Multiclass classification is when there are more than two classes to which data objects can belong. Multiclass classification is an extension of binary classification; thus, usually, to evaluate performance in multiclass classification, several averaging techniques across classes are used, for example, macro or weighted averaging. The typical approach in multiclass classification task is to consider each class as a binary case based on One-vs-Rest approaches in which the class under consideration is treated to be a positive one while other negative ones.

glossary.definition: a task where a single processing unit in the output layer classifies something into two classes only; used when the dataset contains values for two input features and we want to classify them into one of two classes.

The binary classification task allows a single processing unit in the output layer to classify something in two classes only.

glossary.definition: It means that after processing the input signals in the whole network, the artificial neural network receives a final output which in the case of supervised machine learning can be compared with the desired (actual label) output.
glossary.sentence: Learning in artificial neural networks is related to modifying weights in some particular way . It means that after processing the input signals in the whole network, the artificial neural network receives a final output which in the case of supervised machine learning can be compared with the desired (actual label) output. In case if there is a difference between the desired output and the output of the network, a learning rule is applied to modify the weights of the network. Therefore, the artificial neural network starts the next processing step with updated weight values.

glossary.definition: Hierarchical clustering is about building a hierarchy of clusters that is characterised by the following aspects (Hastie, 2017) : the clusters at each level of the hierarchy are created by merging/dividing clusters at the next lower level; at the lowest level, each cluster contains a single data object; at the highest level, there is only one cluster containing all of the data objects.
glossary.sentence: Hierarchical clustering Hierarchical clustering is about building a hierarchy of clusters that is characterised by the following aspects (Hastie, 2017) : the clusters at each level of the hierarchy are created by merging/dividing clusters at the next lower level; at the lowest level, each cluster contains a single data object; at the highest level, there is only one cluster containing all of the data objects.

glossary.definition: Agglomerative hierarchical clustering is a bottom-up approach to building a hierarchy of clusters. Therefore, each data object initially is attributed to its own cluster. Then, at each level, a selected pair of clusters is recursively merged into a single cluster. This produces a grouping at the next higher level with one less cluster. The pairs chosen for merging consist of the two groups with the smallest intergroup dissimilarity (the fragment is based on the information given in (Hastie, 2017)) . Therefore, a pair of clusters is merged at each hierarchy level until one cluster with all data objects is acquired at the highest level.
