glossary.definition: True positives (TP) – a data object is positive (label 1), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 1). For example, an e-mail is classified as spam, and it is spam.
glossary.sentence: Predictions made in the classification task can lead to four types of outcomes (Kubat, 2017) : Correct predictions: True positives (TP) – a data object is positive (label 1), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 1). For example, an e-mail is classified as spam, and it is spam.

glossary.definition: True negatives (TN) - a data object is negative (label 0), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 0). For example, an e-mail is classified as non-spam, and it is non-spam.
glossary.sentence: Predictions made in the classification task can lead to four types of outcomes (Kubat, 2017) : Correct predictions: True negatives (TN) - a data object is negative (label 0), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 0). For example, an e-mail is classified as non-spam, and it is non-spam.

glossary.definition: False positives (FP) – a data object is negative (label 0), but the algorithm labels it as positive (label 1); for example, an e-mail is classified as spam, but it is not. In this case, we can say that a data object is “falsely” predicted as positive.
glossary.sentence: Erroneous predictions: False positives (FP) – a data object is negative (label 0), but the algorithm labels it as positive (label 1); for example, an e-mail is classified as spam, but it is not. In this case, we can say that a data object is “falsely” predicted as positive.

glossary.definition: False negatives (FN) – a data object is positive (label 1), but the classifier labels it as negative (label 0), for example, an e-mail is classified as non-spam, but it is spam. In this case, we can say that a data object is “falsely” predicted as negative.
glossary.sentence: Erroneous predictions: False negatives (FN) – a data object is positive (label 1), but the classifier labels it as negative (label 0), for example, an e-mail is classified as non-spam, but it is spam. In this case, we can say that a data object is “falsely” predicted as negative.

glossary.definition: It is a two-dimensional table of counts of how often each class/category is classified or misclassified as each other category (Russell & Norvig. 2010) . Thus, it represents detailed information on the number of correct and erroneous predictions for each class/category.
glossary.sentence: The mentioned outcomes are typically summarised in a confusion matrix (Kubat, 2017) . It is a two-dimensional table of counts of how often each class/category is classified or misclassified as each other category (Russell & Norvig. 2010) . glossary.definition: In multiclass classification, the main diagonal of the confusion matrix represents the number of data objects correctly predicted (true positives for a specific class/category).
glossary.sentence: In multiclass classification, the main diagonal of the confusion matrix represents the number of data objects correctly predicted (true positives for a specific class/category). Still, other cells correspond to the number of data objects mislabelled by the algorithm.

glossary.definition: Taking a specific class/category, we can consider the values at the intersections of the column of the corresponding class with other classes as false positives.
glossary.sentence: Thus, we can conclude that an incorrect prediction of the positive class is made, or, in other words, a real class of the data object is negative. The mentioned outcomes are typically summarised in a confusion matrix (Kubat, 2017) . It is a two-dimensional table of counts of how often each class/category is classified or misclassified as each other category (Russell & Norvig. 2010) . Thus, it represents detailed information on the number of correct and erroneous predictions for each class/category. Each row in a confusion matrix represents an actual class, while each column represents a predicted class (Geron, 2017) . The structure of the matrix differs a little bit for the binary classification task and multiclass classification. Table 1 represents the structure of the confusion matrix for the binary classification, while Table 2 gives an example of the matrix. In multiclass classification, the main diagonal of the confusion matrix represents the number of data objects correctly predicted (true positives for a specific class/category). Still, other cells correspond to the number of data objects mislabelled by the algorithm. Taking a specific class/category, we can consider the values at the intersections of the column of the corresponding class with other classes as false positives, while values at the intersections of the row of the corresponding class with other classes as false negatives.

glossary.definition: The typical approach is to consider each class as a binary case and evaluate performance using averaging techniques across classes such as macro or weighted averaging. However, the text does not define accuracy explicitly; rather it mentions that 'macro or weighted averaging' are used.
glossary.sentence: Multiclass classification evaluation uses averaging techniques across classes for example, macro or weighted averaging.

glossary.definition: a classifier is a model used to detect or classify data objects; for instance, training a classifier to detect videos that are safe for kids, or detecting shoplifters on surveillance images.
glossary.sentence: Geron (2017) gives the following example: “if you trained a classifier to detect videos that are safe for kids, ...

glossary.definition: the proportion of true positive cases among all actual positives; in the text, it is mentioned as preferring high precision over recall when detecting safe videos, while in shoplifting detection, 99% recall is desired even if precision is low.
glossary.sentence: if you trained a classifier to detect videos that are safe for kids, you would probably prefer a classifier that rejects many good videos (low recall) but keeps only safe ones (high precision), ...

glossary.definition: the proportion of true positive cases among the predicted positives; in the text, it is mentioned as keeping only safe videos for kids and tolerating lower recall, or having low precision with high recall when detecting shoplifters.
glossary.sentence: if you trained a classifier to detect videos that are safe for kids, you would probably prefer a classifier that rejects many good videos (low recall) but keeps only safe ones (high precision), ...

glossary.definition: the phenomenon where increasing precision reduces recall and vice versa; it is mentioned in the text as an unavoidable aspect of classifier performance.
glossary.sentence: Unfortunately, you can't have it both ways: increasing precision reduces recall and vice versa. This is called the precision/recall tradeoff .

glossary.definition: a metric used in classification tasks that represents the harmonic mean of precision and recall; a high F1 score indicates that both precision and recall are high.
glossary.sentence: The F1 score is one more metric used in the classification tasks. It represents the harmonic mean of precision and recall because the classifier will only get a high F1 score if both recall and precision are high (Geron, 2017) .

glossary.definition: in multiclass classification, macro F1 is calculated by taking the average of precision and recall across classes.
glossary.sentence: In the multiclass classification task, macro F1 can be calculated by the macro average precision and recall.

glossary.definition: a plot that visualizes the classifier's performance by plotting false positive rate (x-axis) versus true positive rate (y-axis) for different parameter values; it helps in selecting good tradeoff points.
glossary.sentence: The behaviour of the classifier under different values of its parameters can be visualised by the so-called ROC (The receiver operating characteristic) curve (Kubat, 2017) .

glossary.definition: the area under the ROC curve; it provides a single-number summary of the classifier's performance, with values in the range [0,1], where 1 is highly accurate and 0.5 indicates random chance.
glossary.sentence: Area under the ROC Curve (AUC) allows comparing different classifiers. It provides a single-number summary of the ROC curve ...

glossary.definition: a machine learning task that involves dividing data objects into representative groups without using labeled data.
glossary.sentence: Metrics for clustering tasks In general, the clustering task demands dividing data objects into representative groups. Thus, its output is groups of data objects.

glossary.definition: a metric used to evaluate the performance of clustering that does not require ground-truth labels; it is calculated based on average intra-cluster distance (a) and inter-cluster distance (b), representing how close each data object in a cluster is to objects in neighboring clusters, with values ranging from -1 to 1.
glossary.sentence: One of the metrics that do not demand ground-truth labels is the Silhouette Score . The Silhouette Score is calculated by the following way: where a - average intra-cluster distance (the average distance between each point within a cluster), b - average inter-cluster distance (the average distance between all clusters).

glossary.definition: a plot that represents clustering performance, with the y-axis showing cluster labels and the x-axis showing Silhouette Scores; the size of the silhouettes is proportional to the number of data objects in each cluster.
glossary.sentence: The Silhouette Scores are usually plotted on the so-called Silhouette Plot , representing the cluster label on the y-axis and the Silhouette Score on the x-axis (Fig.6).

glossary.definition: a cell in the human brain that consists of a soma containing a nucleus, dendrites (extensions receiving signals), and an axon (which transmits signals to other neurons through synapses).
glossary.sentence: Biological neuron The human brain consists of cells called neurons. Each neuron has a cell body (a soma) containing a nucleus ...

glossary.definition: root-like extensions from the neuron's cell body that serve to receive signals from other neurons.
glossary.sentence: Each neuron has a cell body (a soma) containing a nucleus and two types of root-like extensions from the cell body: many dendrites and a single axon ...

glossary.definition: the single extension from the neuron that transmits signals to other neurons through synapses.
glossary.sentence: Each neuron has a cell body (a soma) containing a nucleus and two types of root-like extensions from the cell body: many dendrites and a single axon ...

glossary.definition: a synapse in neural networks is associated with learning; its strength (or conductivity) increases if it carries more electrical current, and decreases if used infrequently or not active at all. Stronger means that the synapse has a higher conductivity.
glossary.sentence: Learning in human neural networks is associated with synapses. It is known that a synapse is made stronger by the however much more electrical current it must carry. Stronger here means that the synapse has a higher conductivity. Synapses that are used often obtain an increasingly higher weight. For synapses that are used infrequently or are not active at all, the conductivity continues to decrease. This can even lead to them dying off (the fragment is adopted from (Ertel, 2011)).

glossary.definition: synapses that promote firing in the receiving neuron.
glossary.sentence: Some of the signals reaching the neuron's dendrites promote firing, and others inhibit it. These are called excitatory and inhibitory synapses, respectively.

glossary.definition: synapses that inhibit or reduce the likelihood of firing in the receiving neuron.
glossary.sentence: Some of the signals reaching the neuron's dendrites promote firing, and others inhibit it. These are called excitatory and inhibitory synapses, respectively.

glossary.definition: an excitatory synapse is described as having a positive weight and an inhibitory synapse a negative weight, then we can calculate the strength of each synapse (by multiplying the strength of the incoming signal by the corresponding synaptic weight)
glossary.sentence: If we think of an excitatory synapse as having a positive weight and an inhibitory synapse a negative weight, then we can calculate the strength of each synapse (by multiplying the strength of the incoming signal by the corresponding synaptic weight). Adding all the synapses together gives the total strength of the signals received at the synapses – and hence the total input to the neuron.

glossary.definition: an inhibitory synapse is described as having a negative weight and when calculating synaptic strength, multiplying the strength of the incoming signal by the corresponding synaptic weight
glossary.sentence: If we think of an excitatory synapse as having a positive weight and an inhibitory synapse a negative weight, then we can calculate the strength of each synapse (by multiplying the strength of the incoming signal by the corresponding synaptic weight). Adding all the synapses together gives the total strength of the signals received at the synapses – and hence the total input to the neuron.

glossary.definition: the synaptic weight is used in calculating the strength of a synapse by multiplying it with the incoming signal's strength. It can be positive (for excitatory) or negative (for inhibitory).
glossary.sentence: If we think of an excitatory synapse as having a positive weight and an inhibitory synapse a negative weight, then we can calculate the strength of each synapse (by multiplying the strength of the incoming signal by the corresponding synaptic weight).

glossary.definition: a neuron receives input signals from synapses; if the total input exceeds its threshold, it will fire. It is described as a computing unit that processes electrochemical activity in networks (biological) or numerical values in artificial neurons.
glossary.sentence: If this total input exceeds the threshold of the neuron, then the neuron will fire (the fragment is adopted from (Bermúdez, 2014)). Therefore, the human mental activity consists primarily of electrochemical activity in networks of neurons (Russell & Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: consists primarily of electrochemical activity in networks of neurons.

glossary.definition: the human brain is described as a highly complex, non-linear and parallel processing system that processes information simultaneously throughout the whole network rather than at a specific location.
glossary.sentence: According to ( Negnevitsky , 2011), the human brain is a highly complex, non-linear and parallel processing system that processes information simultaneously throughout the whole network rather than at a specific location.

glossary.definition: a mathematical model of a biological neuron defined by McCulloch and Pitts in 1943; it has one or more inputs represented by numerical values, real-valued weights that express the strength/importance of each input, an output represented numerically, a function for combining input signals (typically summation) and possibly other functions, as well as a transfer (activation) function and bias input. The bias input provides an offset from the origin and is typically set to one.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neuron In 1943, McCulloch and Pitts defined a mathematical model of the neuron (Ertel, 2011; Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011), which laid fundamentals for artificial neural networks. In general, an artificial neuron has one or more inputs (sometimes also called input signals) which are represented by numerical values that enter from the environment or are delivered by other neurons. The numerical values from the environment typically correspond to the values of data object features in the dataset. The inputs typically are labelled by indexed x; a set of real-valued weights (one for each input) that express the strength, or, in other words, the importance of each neuron input in terms of its influence on the output. Weights are the basic means of long-term memory in artificial neural networks. They are determined in the process of neural network learning. Weights are typically represented by indexed w; an output that is represented by a numerical value and can be either a final solution of the artificial neural network or output to other neurons. It is usually represented by indexed y; a function for combining input signals and providing a single numerical value. It is typically a summation function that sums up the weighted values of inputs. However, other functions are also allowed; a transfer (activation) function that determines the output of the neuron and allows the neural network to model complex non-linear relationships; a bias input which is also commonly applied, and it provides the offset of the line/hyperplane from the origin. If no bias existed, the line (or the hyperplane) would be restricted to pass through the origin, and the weights would only control the angle of the discriminant. The bias is commonly set to one, but the learning algorithm of an artificial neural network should tune its weight. The bias input is typically indexed with 0 index.

glossary.definition: Learning in artificial neural networks is related to modifying weights in some particular way . It means that after processing the input signals in the whole network, the artificial neural network receives a final output which in the case of supervised machine learning can be compared with the desired (actual label) output.
glossary.sentence: Learning in artificial neural networks is related to modifying weights in some particular way . It means that after processing the input signals in the whole network, the artificial neural network receives a final output which in the case of supervised machine learning can be compared with the desired (actual label) output. In case if there is a difference between the desired output and the output of the network, a learning rule is applied to modify the weights of the network. Therefore, the artificial neural network starts the next processing step with updated weight values.

glossary.definition: a function that determines the output of the neuron, allowing the neural network to model complex non-linear relationships; common choices include step, sign, linear and sigmoid functions.

glossary.sentence: Each processing unit calculates the weighted sum, including the bias input, and then applies the activation function.

glossary.definition: a basic type of feed-forward neural network that, with one hidden layer, can represent any continuous function of the input signals and with two hidden layers - even discontinuous functions (Negnevitsky, 2011). Therefore, a feed-forward neural network has greater processing power, and it can model practically any function of arbitrary complexity, where the number of inputs and the number of hidden layers determine the function complexity (Jones, 2009).
glossary.sentence: Multilayer Perceptron The multilayer perceptron is a basic type of feed-forward neural network that, with one hidden layer, can represent any continuous function of the input signals and with two hidden layers - even discontinuous functions (Negnevitsky, 2011). Therefore, a feed-forward neural network has greater processing power, and it can model practically any function of arbitrary complexity, where the number of inputs and the number of hidden layers determine the function complexity (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: at least one hidden layer that consists of computing neurons and usually performs the detection of features. The developer defines the number of hidden layers and processing units in them, often through multiple experimentation loops.
glossary.sentence: It has (Negnevitsky, 2011): an input layer that accepts input signals from the environment and sends these signals to hidden layers. It does not include computing neurons; at least one hidden layer that consists of computing neurons and usually performs the detection of features. Neurons in the hidden layer cannot be observed through the input/output behaviour, and there is no obvious way to know what the desired output of the hidden layer should be. The developer defines the number of hidden layers and processing units in them, often through multiple experimentation loops.

glossary.definition: an input layer that accepts input signals from the environment and sends these signals to hidden layers; the input layer is pre-defined by the number of input features.
glossary.sentence: It has (Negnevitsky, 2011): an input layer that accepts input signals from the environment and sends these signals to hidden layers. The input layer is pre-defined by the number of input features.

glossary.definition: an output layer that sends the numerical output produced by the neural network to the environment and consists of computing neurons; the output layer is also pre-defined because the number of its processing units corresponds to the number of classification categories/classes.
glossary.sentence: It has (Negnevitsky, 2011): an input layer ... ; an output layer that sends the numerical output produced by the neural network to the environment and consists of computing neurons; the output layer is also pre-defined because the number of its processing units corresponds to the number of classification categories/classes.

glossary.definition: processing unit that calculates the weighted sum, including the bias input, and then applies the activation function. They are present in hidden layers (which consist of computing neurons) and output layer (which consists of computing neurons).
glossary.sentence: It has (Negnevitsky, 2011): ... an output layer that sends the numerical output produced by the neural network to the environment and consists of computing neurons; at least one hidden layer that consists of computing neurons and usually performs the detection of features.

glossary.definition: each neuron is a processing unit which calculates the weighted sum, including the bias input, and then applies the activation function. In the hidden layer there are processing units that calculate the weighted sum and apply the activation function, and in the output layer there is a processing unit (in binary classification) that classifies something into two classes.
glossary.sentence: Each neuron is connected to all neurons of the next layer. However, neurons at the same layer are not connected. Each processing unit calculates the weighted sum, including the bias input, and then applies the activation function. The hidden layer will have two processing units that will calculate the weighted sum and apply the activation function.

glossary.definition: the process of propagating inputs forward in the network from the input layer to the output layer; during feed-forwarding, all input features are entered into processing units of the hidden layer and then transformed mathematically by applying summation and activation functions.
glossary.sentence: The learning in multilayer perceptron is based on ... performing feed-forwarding – propagation of inputs forward in the network. The inputs are propagated over the network from the input layer to the output layer during the feed-forwarding.

glossary.definition: the process of propagating error backwards in the network from the output layer to previous hidden layers, used for adjusting weights; it includes two main phases: feed-forwarding and backpropagation. The algorithm allows modifying weights in all network layers through propagation of the error from the output layer to the input layer.
glossary.sentence: The learning in multilayer perceptron is based on ... performing backpropagation of error backwards in the network: from the output layer to the previous hidden layer, and from this hidden layer back to a hidden layer before, etc. until reaching the weights of the input layer; ... Therefore, the learning in the multilayer perceptron is based on the backpropagation algorithm.

glossary.definition: the data elements fed into the neural network; for example, x1 and x2 are input features that are received by the input layer along with a bias (x0).
glossary.sentence: Imagine that we have a dataset that contains values for two input features. Moreover, we want to use the neural network for the binary classification task. For this purpose, we can use a very simple multilayer perceptron network that includes an input layer, one hidden layer, and an output layer. The input layer will receive two input features (x1 and x2) and input bias (x0).

glossary.definition: a processing unit in the output layer that calculates a weighted sum including bias and applies an activation function to produce the predicted output value representing a class of the input object.
glossary.sentence: The binary classification task allows a single processing unit in the output layer to classify something in two classes only. ... The resulting value received from the processing unit of the output layer is the predicted output value representing a class of the input object.

glossary.definition: the mathematical process of adding weighted inputs (including bias) performed by each neuron before applying the activation function.
glossary.sentence: Each processing unit calculates the weighted sum, including the bias input, and then applies the activation function.

glossary.definition: the sum of products of inputs and corresponding weights (plus a bias) computed by each neuron before applying the activation function.

glossary.definition: a specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base; works closely with domain experts.
glossary.sentence: However, the knowledge engineer is not a specialist in all problem domains. This is a reason why he/she works in a close collaboration with the domain expert.

glossary.definition: a person who has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain; possesses domain-specific expertise.

glossary.definition: devising a sequence of actions that will achieve a set of goals given certain starting conditions and run-time constraints.
glossary.sentence: Planning - devising a sequence of actions that will achieve a set of goals given certain starting conditions and run-time constraints.

glossary.sentence: Monitoring - comparing a system’s observed behaviour to its expected behaviour (for example, instruction-assisting in the education process in technical domains).

glossary.definition: governing the behaviour of a complex environment.

glossary.definition: means that offer a set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge. Using them, a knowledge representation or description is created.
glossary.sentence: Knowledge representation schemas are means that offer a set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge. Using them, a knowledge representation or description is created.

glossary.definition: represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge.
glossary.sentence: logical knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge;

glossary.definition: represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them.

glossary.definition: Structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge. glossary.definition: theories developed in psychology that postulate that knowledge on different concepts is related by associations in the human mind and these associations define the meaning of a concept in relation to meaning of other concepts.
glossary.sentence: Networked knowledge representation schemas are based on associationist theories of human mind that were developed in psychology. These theories postulate that knowledge on different concepts is related by associations in the human mind and these associations define the meaning of a concept in relation to meaning of other concepts (Luger, 2009), for example, the concept “university” for the most part of students could be associated with such concepts as lectures, students, professors, exams, science etc.

glossary.definition: a knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them.
glossary.sentence: A semantic network is a knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009; Mallach, 1994; Winston, 1992).

glossary.definition: a relation “subclass-class” (also “subset-set”, “subtype-type”, “subkind-kind”) is represented by the title of “is a” (also “is a subclass of”, “is a subset of”, “is a type of” or “is a kind of”). It allows showing hierarchical relations by relating narrower concepts to broader concepts.
glossary.sentence: A relation 'subclass-class' (also 'subset-set', 'subtype-type', 'subkind-kind') is represented by the title of 'is a' (also 'is a subclass of', 'is a subset of', 'is a type of' or 'is a kind of'). It allows showing hierarchical relations by relating narrower concepts to broader concepts (Fig.6).

glossary.definition: includes a set of concepts/objects that share common properties.
glossary.sentence: A class/set/type/kind includes a set of concepts/objects that share common properties.

glossary.definition: a relation 'instance-class/set/type/kind' is represented by the title of 'is an instance/example of'. It also allows creating hierarchical relations by relating one specific object in the problem domain to a class/set/type/kind it belongs to.
glossary.sentence: A relation “instance-class/set/type/kind” is represented by the title of “is an instance/example of”. It also allows creating hierarchical relations by relating one specific object in the problem domain to a class/set/type/kind it belongs to (Fig.7).

glossary.definition: a relation 'Part of' is represented by the title of “Part of”. It allows relating parts of the object to the whole object.
glossary.sentence: A relation 'part-whole' is represented by the title of 'Part of' (Fig.8). It allows relating parts of the object to the whole object.

glossary.sentence: A relation “object-property” is represented by the title of “property/has a property of”. It allows relating objects/concepts to their properties (Fig.9).

glossary.definition: a relation 'property-value' is represented by the title of “value”. It allows relating properties of objects/concepts to their values.
glossary.sentence: A relation “property-value” is represented by the title of “value”. It allows relating properties of objects/concepts to their values (Fig.10). This relation can come only after the relation 'object-property'.

glossary.definition: they allow specifying any other kind of relations (for example, like, own, travel, etc.) that cannot be represented by the previously mentioned types of relations.
glossary.sentence: Linguistic relations (Fig.11). They allow specifying any other kind of relations (for example, like, own, travel, etc.) that cannot be represented by the previously mentioned types of relations.

glossary.definition: the generalization relation ensures inheritance in frames. Therefore, sub-class and instance frames have a possibility to inherit slots and their values from class frames, but they can also contain properties that are not represented in the class frame.
glossary.sentence: Inheritance is an essential feature of frame-based expert systems (Negnevitsky, 2011). The generalization relation ensures inheritance in frames. Therefore, sub-class and instance frames have a possibility to inherit slots and their values from class frames, but they can also contain properties that are not represented in the class frame.

glossary.definition: The inference engine for semantic networks is based on the following principles: to acquire knowledge about some object/concept, explore relations of the object/concept to other objects/concept by following direction of arcs; to find knowledge about a specific object/concept: check local relations of the object/ concept; if knowledge is not found, perform inheritance (if it is possible). Semantic networks have a number of advantages: they allow ease of representation and understanding of knowledge; they allow defining a variety of relations between objects/ concepts; they represent knowledge in a structured way; they support inheritance; they have a simple inference engine. At the same time, semantic networks have disadvantages: they represent only declarative knowledge (knowledge about facts, objects, and concepts ); they do not have well-defined formal semantics and can introduce ambiguity both during the knowledge representation when the same unit of knowledge can be represented in different ways, and during the interpretation of a semantic network, when the content represented can be interpreted in different ways; they do not allow to represent specific types of knowledge like negation, disjunction, or also all/part/several/nobody/many, etc.

Semantic networks have a number of advantages: they allow ease of representation and understanding of knowledge; they allow defining a variety of relations between objects/ concepts; they represent knowledge in a structured way; they support inheritance; they have a simple inference engine. At the same time, semantic networks have disadvantages: they represent only declarative knowledge (knowledge about facts, objects, and concepts ); they do not have well-defined formal semantics and can introduce ambiguity both during the knowledge representation when the same unit of knowledge can be represented in different ways, and during the interpretation of a semantic network, when the content represented can be interpreted in different ways; they do not allow to represent specific types of knowledge like negation, disjunction, or also all/part/several/nobody/many, etc.

The inference engine for semantic networks is based on the following principles: to acquire knowledge about some object/concept, explore relations of the object/concept to other objects/concept by following direction of arcs; to find knowledge about a specific object/concept: check local relations of the object/ concept; if knowledge is not found, perform inheritance (if it is possible).
Explore relations of the object/concept to other objects/concept by following direction of arcs.
Follow the direction of arcs when exploring relations in semantic networks.
To find knowledge about a specific object/concept: check local relations of the object/ concept.

Structured knowledge representation schemas use a graph to represent knowledge. However, comparing to the networked knowledge representation schemas, each node in the graph represents not a single concept/object, but a complex structure describing an object/concept together with its properties and parts.
A complex structure describing an object/concept together with its properties and parts in structured knowledge representation schemas.
Each node represents a complex structure that describes an object/concept together with its properties and parts.
Each node in the graph represents not only the concept/object but also its parts as part of the complex structure describing an object/concept.

In structured knowledge representation schemas, relations are represented by a referencing principle when one node references another node by its name instead of representing them explicitly with arcs.

Frames are an example of structured knowledge representation schemas. Marvin Minsky developed frames in 1975. They are based on the following idea: “When one encounters a new situation (or makes a substantial change in one's view of the present problem) one selects from memory a structure called a Frame. This is a remembered framework to be adapted to fit reality by changing details as necessary.” (Minsky, 1975)
A data-structure for representing a stereotyped situation. It represents typical knowledge about an object/concept or information relevant to stereotyped entities.
An example of a frame is the credit application form which demonstrates how frames work in structured representations.
The frame has a specific structure (Fig.2) that includes: slot titles, slot values and facets.
Each frame has a specific structure that includes slot titles, slot values and facets.

Each frame has a specific structure that includes slot titles, slot values and facets. Each property (or slot) has a value that can be represented as a symbolic, numerical or boolean value, as well as it can be empty or can contain a title of another frame.

Facets are an extension to the slot-value structure of frames as they represent extended knowledge about slot values and tell the inference engine how to process the property of the object, allow to set a slot value and control user's access to the slot value. Facets can contain value type, value constraints (maximum value, minimum value, range of values, etc.), and actions that can be performed with slot values.
Different sources can provide slot values: initialization – many slot values are assigned during the creation of the knowledge base.
Different sources can provide slot values: external programs – nowadays intelligent systems communicate with external programs like databases or expert systems that can deliver slot values.
It is possible to assign procedures and methods to slots that, for example, will calculate a slot value when it is needed (see further in the text ).
Different sources can provide slot values: user – the intelligent system can demand the user to provide slot values during the system’s operation.
Frames support inheritance of slots and their values. Using this type of frames, we can also create sub-classes that are classes derived from other classes.

There are two main types of frames: A class/generic frame. It contains common properties of a set/class/kind/type of objects/concepts. The class frames help us to represent a problem in an abstract form.

A class is a type of frame that contains common properties of a set/class/kind/type of objects/concepts and helps represent a problem in an abstract form. Typically, a class frame includes a title of the class/set/type/kind, a set of common properties, and values of properties that are common to all objects of the class.
Using this type of frames, we can also create sub-classes that are classes derived from other classes. The sub-class frames can contain new slots that are not included in the parent frame.
A frame represents typical knowledge about an object/concept or information relevant to stereotyped entities.
Semantic networks do not allow to represent specific types of knowledge such as negation, disjunction or also/part/several/nobody.
the class frames allow creating multi-level hierarchies of class and sub-class frames.
glossary.sentence: As a result, the class frames allow creating multi-level hierarchies of class and sub-class frames.

glossary.definition: an instance frame represents a detailed knowledge about a particular object in the problem domain
glossary.sentence: An instance frame. It represents a detailed knowledge about a particular object in the problem domain (Figure 6). Typically the instance frames include all slot values and are derived from the class/sub-class frames.

glossary.definition: slots are used to store values in frames, including those not defined for class/sub-class frames. They represent what kind of slot values is stored in the frame.
glossary.sentence: However, instance frames can also contain slots that are not defined for class/sub-class frames. These are the frames that an intelligent computer system uses to solve problems. A referencing principle is used to relate two frames. This means that a unique title of one frame is used as a slot value in another frame (Figure 7). Therefore, frames are related only by slot values, not by slot titles , because slot titles explain what kind of slot values is stored in the frame.

glossary.definition: it relates a sub-class frame with a class frame or an instance frame with a sub-class/class frame. It allows making hierarchies of frames where the most general frame is located at the highest level and the most specific ones – at the lower levels.
glossary.sentence: Three types of relations are used to relate frames (Negnevitsky, 2011): Generalization relation . It relates a sub-class frame with a class frame or an instance frame with a sub-class/class frame. It allows making hierarchies of frames where the most general frame is located at the highest level and the most specific ones – at the lower levels.

glossary.definition: it allows representing extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts; frames corresponding to the components of an object are associated with a frame representing the object as a whole.
glossary.sentence: Aggregation relation . It allows representing extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts (Figure 9). In other words, frames corresponding to the components of an object are associated with a frame representing the object as a whole.

glossary.definition: it allows defining any other type of relations by using the referencing principle.
glossary.sentence: Association or semantic relation . It allows defining any other type of relations by using the referencing principle (Figure 10).

glossary.definition: when a frame inherits slots and slot values from several other frames simultaneously is allowed if slot titles of parent frames are unique.
glossary.sentence: Moreover, frames support overwriting of the inherited values. Multiple inheritance when a frame inherits slots and slot values from several other frames simultaneously is allowed if slot titles of parent frames are unique.

glossary.definition: a slot can have a procedural attachment, which means it is possible to add regular procedures and/or demons.
glossary.sentence: A slot can have a procedural attachment (Negnevitsky, 2011). In general, it is possible to add regular procedures and/or demons.

glossary.definition: a self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system used for various purposes like generating an image of the object, calculating numerical values, acquiring data from external sources, etc.
glossary.sentence: A regular procedure is a self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system (Negnevitsky, 2011). We can use such procedures for different purposes like generating an image of the object, calculating some numerical values, such as the amount of taxes to be paid, acquiring data from external sources, etc.

glossary.definition: a demon is a procedure that is invoked as a side effect of some other action in the knowledge base. Demons are triggered automatically when a slot value is accessed and control actions for slot-level manipulations.
glossary.sentence: Demons implement actions in a frame-based expert system (Jones, 2009; Luger,2009; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992). A demon is a procedure that is invoked as a side effect of some other action in the knowledge base (Luger, 2009). Demons are triggered automatically when a slot value is accessed (Mallach, 1994). Thus, demons control actions for slot-level manipulations (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: the common demons defined for frames include IF-NEEDED – triggered when the slot value is unspecified and necessary to calculate/set it; IF-CHANGED – triggered when a slot value changes; IF-ADDED – triggered when a slot value is unspecified and added to the frame; IF-REMOVED – triggered when a slot value is deleted.
glossary.sentence: The common demons are: IF-NEEDED – it is triggered when the slot value is unspecified, and it is necessary to calculate/to set it; IF-CHANGED – it is triggered when a slot value changes; IF-ADDED – it is triggered when a slot value is unspecified, and it is added to the frame; IF-REMOVED – it is triggered when a slot value is deleted.

glossary.definition: frames represent associative relations between frames, such as Owns and Animal owner, which define relationships like BrownLucie - Tom (Owns) and Tom-BrownLucie (Animal owner), TreeAlex - Rex (Owns) and Rex-TreeAlex (Animal owner), and Tom – Rex (Afraid of).
glossary.sentence: The frame system also represents the following associative relations between frames: BrownLucie - Tom , the relation Owns , and Tom- BrownLucie , the relation Animal owner; TreeAlex - Rex , the relation Owns , and Rex- TreeAlex , the relation Animal owner; Tom – Rex , the relation Afraid of .

glossary.definition: frames provide hierarchical organization of knowledge by establishing class frames, sub-class frames, instance frames, and relations such as generalization and aggregation.
glossary.sentence: Frames store knowledge about each object in one place in a structured way; frames provide the hierarchical organization of knowledge.
